@flint v0 hybrid
G: split_monolith_preserving_order_payment_consistency
C: team("8_eng") ∧ runway("24mo") ∧ budget("6mo_migration") ∧ stack("postgres_kafka_redis_k8s") ∧ no_distributed_tx ∧ strong_consistency("order_to_payment") ∧ pain("release_coupling_12min_tests_migration_blocks")
P: services("order_payment_merged__fulfillment__inventory__plus_gateway_bff") ∧ db_per_service("own_schema_own_migrations_no_cross_joins") ∧ sync("order_to_payment_grpc_single_pg_tx") ∧ async("kafka_domain_events_avro_schema_registry") ∧ outbox_pattern("debezium_logical_decoding_no_dual_writes") ∧ saga("fulfillment_inventory_compensating_with_idempotency_keys") ∧ inventory_reservation("redis_ttl_soft_hold_confirm_on_payment") ∧ shared_data_rule("owner_writes_others_read_via_event_projection")
V: ci_per_service("lt_3min") ∧ deploy_independence("no_cross_team_merge_conflicts") ∧ order_payment_atomicity("single_pg_tx") ∧ event_lag("p99_lt_5s") ∧ saga_compensation_rate("lt_0_5pct") ∧ no_dual_writes("outbox_only") ∧ shadow_traffic_then_cutover ∧ rollback("feature_flag_per_route")
A: strangler_fig_gateway("envoy_route_by_endpoint") ∧ extract_order_payment("month_1_2") ∧ extract_inventory("month_3") ∧ extract_fulfillment("month_4_5") ∧ decommission_monolith("month_6") ∧ adopt_outbox_relay("debezium") ∧ define_event_contracts("avro_schema_registry")

[AUDIT]
Keep order+payment in one service with a single Postgres transaction — preserves strong consistency without distributed tx. Inventory and fulfillment are separate services with their own DBs. Cross-service writes go through the transactional outbox: commit locally, Debezium publishes to Kafka. Inventory uses Redis TTL soft-holds confirmed by saga on payment success, compensated on failure. No cross-service joins — replicate read models via event projections. Sync gRPC only on order→payment hot path; everything else async Kafka with Avro schema registry and idempotency keys. Strangler-fig at an Envoy gateway: extract order+payment (months 1–2), inventory (3), fulfillment (4–5), decommission monolith (6). Rollback via per-route feature flags.

Inventory uses Redis TTL soft-holds for reservation, confirmed via saga on payment success, compensated on failure. No cross-service joins — replicate read models via event projections. Sync gRPC only on the order→payment hot path; everything else async Kafka with Avro schema registry. Strangler-fig migration: extract order+payment first (months 1–2), inventory (3), fulfillment (4–5), decommission (6). Rollback via per-route feature flags at the gateway.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: data_ownership_split_one_owner_per_table
C: services("order_payment__fulfillment__inventory__customer") ∧ tables("orders__order_items__payments__shipments__inventory_holds__customers") ∧ no_distributed_tx ∧ no_cross_service_joins ∧ no_cross_service_writes ∧ strong_consistency("order_to_payment") ∧ transport("grpc_sync_or_kafka_async")
P: order_payment_owns("orders__order_items__payments__payment_attempts__outbox") ∧ fulfillment_owns("shipments__shipment_events__outbox") ∧ inventory_owns("stock_levels__inventory_holds__stock_ledger__outbox") ∧ customer_owns("customers__addresses__outbox") ∧ order_payment_reads("customer_profile_grpc_sync__inventory_hold_ack_kafka_async") ∧ fulfillment_reads("order_snapshot_and_shipping_address_via_kafka_projection") ∧ inventory_reads("order_line_items_via_kafka_for_hold_commands") ∧ customer_reads("none_source_of_truth_no_upstream")
V: unique_owner_invariant("ci_lint_blocks_cross_schema_fk") ∧ pii_isolation("customer_service_only_writes") ∧ order_payment_atomicity("single_pg_tx_same_db") ∧ hold_lifecycle("saga_not_2pc") ∧ projection_freshness("p99_lt_5s") ∧ outbox_only("no_direct_kafka_produce") ∧ read_contracts("consumers_pin_schema_version") ∧ acl("db_grants_writes_to_owner_only")
A: publish_order_payment("order_created__order_confirmed__payment_authorized__payment_captured__order_cancelled") ∧ publish_fulfillment("shipment_created__shipment_dispatched__shipment_delivered__shipment_failed") ∧ publish_inventory("hold_requested__hold_placed__hold_confirmed__hold_released__stock_adjusted") ∧ publish_customer("customer_created__customer_updated__address_changed") ∧ enforce_avro_schema_registry("per_topic_compat_backward") ∧ enforce_outbox_cdc("debezium_logical_decoding") ∧ grpc_contract("customer_get_by_id__customer_verify_address")

[AUDIT]
Four services, each owning a disjoint slice of tables. Order-Payment owns orders, order_items, payments, payment_attempts — merged in one DB so the order→payment commit is a single Postgres transaction. Fulfillment owns shipments and shipment_events. Inventory owns stock_levels, inventory_holds, and stock_ledger. Customer owns customers and addresses (PII isolated). Every service has its own outbox table driven by Debezium CDC — no direct Kafka produce, no dual writes. Reads: Order-Payment calls Customer over gRPC synchronously on checkout for address/profile verification, and consumes Inventory hold acknowledgements off Kafka. Fulfillment and Inventory never call sync — they project order snapshots locally from Kafka events. Customer is a pure source of truth with no upstream reads. Events published: Order-Payment emits order/payment lifecycle; Fulfillment emits shipment lifecycle; Inventory emits hold and stock lifecycle; Customer emits identity and address changes. Schema compatibility enforced via Avro registry (backward). Ownership invariant enforced in CI by a linter that blocks cross-schema foreign keys, and at the database layer by grants that only permit the owner to write.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: saga_order_to_payment_idempotent_no_2pc_no_locks
C: orchestrator("order_payment_service_as_saga_coordinator") ∧ saga_state_store("pg_table_in_order_payment_db") ∧ transport("kafka_commands_and_events_at_least_once") ∧ idempotency_key("saga_id_plus_step_id") ∧ no_2pc ∧ no_distributed_locks ∧ retry_policy("exp_backoff_jitter_max_5_attempts") ∧ deadlines("per_step_timeout_plus_saga_watchdog")
P: states("created__hold_pending__held__auth_pending__authorized__capturing__captured__confirmed__dispatched__compensating__cancelled__failed") ∧ step1_reserve_inventory("cmd_hold_requested_to_inventory__await_hold_placed__timeout_30s_then_fail") ∧ step2_authorize_payment("cmd_authorize_to_psp_via_payment_module__await_authorized__timeout_20s_then_void_and_release") ∧ step3_commit_order("single_pg_tx_update_state_confirmed_plus_insert_outbox_capture_cmd_atomic") ∧ step4_capture_payment("psp_capture_with_idempotency_key__retry_until_captured_or_max__timeout_60s") ∧ step5_emit_order_confirmed("fulfillment_and_inventory_project_from_kafka__inventory_converts_hold_to_commit") ∧ step6_fulfillment_dispatch("shipment_created_emitted__partial_shipments_tracked_per_line") ∧ watchdog("scheduler_scans_sagas_past_deadline_emits_timeout_event")
V: compensate_hold("release_hold_on_auth_fail_or_capture_fail_via_cmd_hold_release") ∧ compensate_auth("void_authorization_if_not_captured") ∧ compensate_capture("issue_refund_if_already_captured_and_downstream_failed") ∧ compensate_order("transition_to_cancelled_emit_order_cancelled") ∧ compensate_fulfillment("cancel_unshipped_lines__rma_restock_shipped_lines_with_partial_refund") ∧ inventory_released_after_payment("if_ttl_expired_before_capture__refund_full__notify_customer__cancel") ∧ payment_timeout_path("void_auth__release_hold__cancel_order_emit_order_failed") ∧ dedup("inbox_unique_idx_on_message_id__psp_idempotency_key_prevents_double_charge")
A: outbox_per_service("state_change_and_event_in_same_local_tx") ∧ inbox_dedup_table("unique_msg_id_on_consume") ∧ psp_idempotency_key("saga_id_colon_attempt_number") ∧ hold_ttl("inventory_ttl_gt_auth_plus_capture_window_plus_slack") ∧ saga_log("append_only_event_sourced_transitions_for_replay") ∧ dlq("poison_events_quarantined_with_manual_review_runbook") ∧ partial_fulfillment_line_sub_saga("per_line_item_saga_rolls_up_to_parent") ∧ runbooks("oncall_playbooks_for_stuck_sagas_and_psp_mismatch")

[AUDIT]
The Order-Payment service is the saga coordinator. State is persisted in its own Postgres DB as an append-only saga log, allowing deterministic replay. States: created → hold_pending → held → auth_pending → authorized → capturing → captured → confirmed → dispatched, with compensating and cancelled as terminal rollback paths. Each step uses a saga_id + step_id idempotency key, consumers dedup via an inbox unique index, and the PSP call uses saga_id:attempt as its idempotency header so retries never double-charge. Step 3 is the strong-consistency anchor: the order state transition to confirmed and the outbox row for the capture command are written in one local Postgres transaction — no 2PC needed. Compensations: auth failure releases the hold and voids; capture failure after auth voids and releases; capture success followed by fulfillment failure issues a refund and restock; inventory TTL expiring before capture triggers a full refund, cancellation, and customer notification. Partial fulfillment uses a per-line sub-saga that rolls up to the parent — shipped lines go RMA/restock with partial refund, unshipped lines cancel. A watchdog scans past-deadline sagas and emits timeout events, which drive the compensation transitions. Poison messages land in a DLQ with an oncall runbook. No distributed locks anywhere — concurrency is serialized per saga_id via the saga_log unique step index.

content: Acknowledged — no active multi-step task to track. Awaiting next turn.

input.audit: Eight user-visible staleness windows and their mitigations. Order history lag: after confirm, the list projection may trail — mitigate by writing the order_list projection row inside the same Postgres transaction as the confirm (Order-Payment owns both), target p99 < 500ms. Stock display on the PDP: projection lag can show "available" after sold out — accept up to 2s on PDP but force an authoritative gRPC check at add-to-cart and again at hold. Payment status: UI "pending" after a successful capture is the worst UX sin — push via SSE/WS from the BFF on payment_captured, with optimistic UI on the 200 from the capture API and a rollback path if a later event reverses it; p99 < 1s. Shipment visibility: order is confirmed but shipment row hasn't landed — use progressive disclosure ("preparing to ship" → "shipped") with a 10s poll fallback; 5s bound. Address change collision: the order snapshot fixes the shipping address; allow edits only until fulfillment reaches "picked" and lock thereafter — pre-picked edits emit a change event that rewrites the snapshot. Refund visibility: in-app refund event lands immediately, but the bank settles in 3–5 business days — clear copy + reconciliation banner. Cart cross-tab: broadcastchannel between tabs plus a WS delta from the BFF; Redis TTL hold remains source of truth; p99 < 500ms. Read-your-write on the BFF: sticky session to primary for 60s after a write, or route the recent writer directly to the owning service rather than a replica.

content:
Over the next six months, we are restructuring the orders platform from a single Django application into four focused services — one combined order-and-payment service, plus dedicated services for fulfillment, inventory, and customer data. The core promise of the current system, that a customer's order and payment are processed together reliably, is preserved by keeping those two domains in the same service and the same database transaction. Everything else communicates through durable event streams, so teams can ship independently without coordinating releases. The move directly addresses the problems slowing us down today: release coupling across four teams, frequent merge conflicts, a twelve-minute test suite that blocks every change, and database migrations that stall deployments. After the migration, each team ships on its own schedule with its own tests, the order-to-payment guarantee remains intact, and the engineering throughput of the eight-person team increases materially.

The principal risk is execution complexity during the transition — we will be running the old monolith and the new services side by side, and a poorly managed cutover could produce visible inconsistencies such as a delayed order appearing in a customer's history or a stale stock count. We mitigate this with a gradual, route-by-route migration behind a gateway that lets us roll any service back instantly, shadow traffic on each service before cutting it over, and concrete freshness targets for every customer-facing screen (for example, an order must appear in history within 500 milliseconds of confirmation). The rollout is sequenced to retire risk early: order and payment in months one and two, inventory in month three, fulfillment in months four and five, and the monolith decommissioned in month six. This fits comfortably inside our two-year runway and leaves the team on a platform that can absorb the next year of product work without re-architecting again.

input.audit: Five day-1 SLOs cover the split's failure modes. One: saga success rate — the end-to-end order-to-payment completion ratio, which is the business-critical guarantee; page at <99.5% over five minutes, or on a 14.4× fast burn. Two: confirm-API latency — p99 < 1.5s, p50 < 300ms, because the split adds a network hop between order and inventory. Three: projection lag from outbox through Kafka into read models — p99 < 5s per topic; this is the earliest indicator that async replication is bottlenecked and user-visible freshness SLOs will follow. Four: saga compensation rate — a rising compensation rate means real orders are failing somewhere and being unwound; page above 0.5% of orders over 15 minutes. Five: per-service 5xx error budget at 0.5% over 5 minutes. Canary-vs-control is a per-endpoint comparison, with traffic labeled at the gateway so Prometheus queries can split the same endpoint by canary vs control and bootstrap a 95% CI over success rate, p99 latency, and compensation rate; auto-rollback if canary is worse than control by 2σ on any SLO for three consecutive 5-minute windows. Dashboard is grouped into five boards: business (GMV, orders/min, conversion, refunds, cart abandon), saga health (state fanout, compensations, watchdog timeouts, DLQ depth), per-service RED (availability, latency, errors, saturation), data plane (Kafka lag, outbox backlog, Debezium slot lag, projection freshness), and user-visible freshness (order history, payment push, stock PDP, shipment). Every alert hyperlinks to a runbook whose top line is the rollback command. Non-critical deploys frozen for the first 24 hours after cutover.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: audit_jwt_module
C: file("auth/jwt_service.py") ∧ hardcoded_secret ∧ algos_include_none_and_rs256 ∧ verify_exp_disabled ∧ no_iss_aud_kid_checks
P: rank_critical("alg_none_accepted_full_auth_bypass") ∧ rank_critical("alg_confusion_rs256_hs256_shared_secret") ∧ rank_critical("verify_exp_false_tokens_never_expire") ∧ rank_critical("secret_hardcoded_in_source") ∧ rank_high("no_iss_aud_nbf_validation") ∧ rank_medium("role_silent_default_user") ∧ rank_medium("no_kid_or_key_rotation") ∧ rank_low("weak_human_readable_hmac_secret")
V: decode_rejects_alg_none ∧ decode_pins_single_algorithm ∧ expired_token_raises ∧ secret_loaded_from_env_or_kms ∧ iss_aud_claims_enforced
A: remove("none") ∧ split_hs_rs_keys_pin_per_issuer ∧ set("options={'verify_exp':True,'require':['exp','iss','aud','sub']}") ∧ load("SECRET=os.environ['JWT_SECRET']") ∧ decode_with("algorithms=[expected_alg]") ∧ validate("iss,aud,nbf,iat") ∧ drop("get_unverified_header_gate") ∧ fail_closed_on_missing_role

[AUDIT]
Four criticals: alg=none in ALGOS → unsigned tokens accepted; RS256+HS256 both allowed with symmetric SECRET → classic key-confusion (attacker HMACs with RSA public key); verify_exp=False → tokens eternal; secret hardcoded in source. High: no iss/aud/nbf/iat validation, get_unverified_header gate is cosmetic since decode still accepts all ALGOS. Medium: role defaults to 'user' silently, no kid/rotation. Low: weak human-readable HMAC secret. Fix: pin single algorithm per verify call, load secret from env/KMS, enable exp + require iss/aud/sub, drop the header gate, fail closed on missing claims.

Low: weak human-readable HMAC secret. Fix: pin single algorithm per verify call, load secret from env/KMS, enable exp + require iss/aud/sub, drop the header gate, fail closed on missing claims.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: rank_top3_decode_path_attacks
C: decode_accepts_alg_none ∧ secret_in_source_tree ∧ verify_exp_false ∧ same_key_for_hs256_and_rs256
P: rank1_alg_none_forgery_zero_prereq ∧ rank2_hs256_forgery_with_leaked_hardcoded_secret ∧ rank3_expired_or_stolen_token_replay_forever ∧ bonus_alg_confusion_if_rs_pubkey_ever_stored_in_secret
V: pytest("verify(forged_none_token).role=='admin'") ∧ pytest("verify(hs256_forged_with_literal_secret).role=='admin'") ∧ pytest("verify(token_exp_in_past) does not raise")
A: payload1("eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJ2aWN0aW0iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJleHAiOjk5OTk5OTk5OTl9.") ∧ payload2("jwt.encode({'sub':'x','role':'admin','exp':9999999999},'development-secret-do-not-commit','HS256')") ∧ payload3("replay_captured_token_with_exp_in_past_role_admin") ∧ fix("algorithms=['HS256'],options={'verify_exp':True,'require':['exp','iss','aud']}") ∧ fix("SECRET=os.environ['JWT_SECRET']") ∧ fix("remove_none_and_rs256_from_allowlist")

[AUDIT]
Rank 1 — alg=none forgery. Zero prerequisites, zero crypto. Header {"alg":"none"}, body {"role":"admin","exp":9999999999}, empty signature; decode() accepts because 'none' is in ALGOS. Rank 2 — HS256 forgery using the hardcoded SECRET from source. Anyone with repo read (or a leaked commit, Docker layer, pip cache) mints admin tokens at will. Rank 3 — replay of expired or stolen tokens: verify_exp=False means captured Authorization headers work forever, no refresh/rotation protects you. Bonus: if SECRET is ever swapped to hold an RSA public key for RS256 rollout, the dual-algo allowlist enables classic HS/RS key-confusion (attacker HMACs with the public key). Fixes: pin algorithms=['HS256'] per call, enable verify_exp, require exp/iss/aud, load secret from env.

Rank 2 — HS256 forgery using the hardcoded SECRET from source. Anyone with repo read (or a leaked commit, Docker layer, pip cache) mints admin tokens at will. Rank 3 — replay of expired or stolen tokens: verify_exp=False means captured Authorization headers work forever, no refresh/rotation protects you. Bonus: if SECRET is ever swapped to hold an RSA public key for RS256 rollout, the dual-algo allowlist enables classic HS/RS key-confusion (attacker HMACs with the public key). Fixes: pin algorithms=['HS256'] per call, enable verify_exp, require exp/iss/aud, load secret from env.

content:
Caveman: pin HS256 only. Drop none+RS256. Load secrets from env (new + legacy). Grandfather: tokens with `iat < cutoff` verify against legacy secret, skip iss/aud require; tokens at/after cutoff must carry iss/aud/role and verify against new secret. Always enforce exp. Preserve `verify(token_str)->Token` signature.

```python
# auth/jwt_service.py
import os
import time
import jwt
from dataclasses import dataclass

ISSUER = "sigil-auth"
AUDIENCE = "sigil-api"
ALG = "HS256"

SECRET = os.environ["JWT_SECRET"]
LEGACY_SECRET = os.environ.get("JWT_LEGACY_SECRET")

# Unix seconds. Tokens with iat < GRANDFATHER_UNTIL verify against LEGACY_SECRET
# and skip iss/aud/role-required checks. After this instant, only new-format
# tokens are accepted. Set to deploy_time + 30d by the release pipeline.
GRANDFATHER_UNTIL = int(os.environ.get("JWT_GRANDFATHER_UNTIL", "0"))


@dataclass
class Token:
    sub: str
    exp: int
    role: str


def sign(sub: str, role: str, ttl: int = 3600) -> str:
    now = int(time.time())
    if not role:
        raise ValueError("role required")
    payload = {
        "iss": ISSUER,
        "aud": AUDIENCE,
        "sub": sub,
        "role": role,
        "iat": now,
        "nbf": now,
        "exp": now + ttl,
    }
    return jwt.encode(payload, SECRET, algorithm=ALG)


def _decode_strict(token: str) -> dict:
    return jwt.decode(
        token,
        SECRET,
        algorithms=[ALG],
        issuer=ISSUER,
        audience=AUDIENCE,
        options={"require": ["exp", "iat", "nbf", "iss", "aud", "sub", "role"]},
    )


def _decode_legacy(token: str) -> dict:
    if not LEGACY_SECRET:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError("legacy verification disabled")
    payload = jwt.decode(
        token,
        LEGACY_SECRET,
        algorithms=[ALG],
        options={"require": ["exp", "sub"]},
    )
    iat = int(payload.get("iat", 0))
    now = int(time.time())
    if now >= GRANDFATHER_UNTIL:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError("grandfather window closed")
    if iat and iat >= GRANDFATHER_UNTIL:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError("token not within grandfather window")
    return payload


def verify(token: str) -> Token:
    try:
        payload = _decode_strict(token)
    except jwt.InvalidTokenError:
        payload = _decode_legacy(token)

    role = payload.get("role")
    if not role:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError("role missing")
    return Token(sub=payload["sub"], exp=int(payload["exp"]), role=role)


def require_admin(token_str: str) -> Token:
    t = verify(token_str)
    if t.role != "admin":
        raise PermissionError("not admin")
    return t
```

Key changes: `algorithms=[ALG]` pinned per call (kills alg=none + HS/RS confusion); `verify_exp` on by default + explicit `require` list; iss/aud enforced for new tokens; legacy path gated by `GRANDFATHER_UNTIL` and separate `LEGACY_SECRET` so the old in-repo string is rotated out; `role` never silently defaults; `verify(token: str) -> Token` signature preserved.

Caveman: pytest module. Set `JWT_SECRET` before import via autouse fixture + `importlib.reload`. Craft each attack with PyJWT primitives — alg=none handcrafted, RS256 with throwaway key, expired exp, wrong secret, tampered base64 payload. Assert `InvalidTokenError` or subclass on every call.

```python
# tests/test_jwt_service.py
import base64
import importlib
import json
import os
import time

import jwt
import pytest
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization

SECRET = "unit-test-secret-xyz"
LEGACY_SECRET = "unit-test-legacy-abc"


@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def service(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv("JWT_SECRET", SECRET)
    monkeypatch.setenv("JWT_LEGACY_SECRET", LEGACY_SECRET)
    monkeypatch.setenv("JWT_GRANDFATHER_UNTIL", str(int(time.time()) + 86400))
    import auth.jwt_service as svc
    importlib.reload(svc)
    return svc


def _b64u(data: bytes) -> str:
    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(data).rstrip(b"=").decode()


def _craft_none_token(payload: dict) -> str:
    header = _b64u(json.dumps({"alg": "none", "typ": "JWT"}).encode())
    body = _b64u(json.dumps(payload).encode())
    return f"{header}.{body}."


def _valid_payload(role="user", **over):
    now = int(time.time())
    p = {
        "iss": "sigil-auth",
        "aud": "sigil-api",
        "sub": "alice",
        "role": role,
        "iat": now,
        "nbf": now,
        "exp": now + 3600,
    }
    p.update(over)
    return p


def test_alg_none_rejected(service):
    token = _craft_none_token(_valid_payload(role="admin"))
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        service.verify(token)


def test_mismatched_algo_rs256_rejected(service):
    key = rsa.generate_private_key(public_exponent=65537, key_size=2048)
    pem = key.private_bytes(
        serialization.Encoding.PEM,
        serialization.PrivateFormat.PKCS8,
        serialization.NoEncryption(),
    )
    token = jwt.encode(_valid_payload(role="admin"), pem, algorithm="RS256")
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidTokenError):
        service.verify(token)


def test_expired_token_rejected(service):
    now = int(time.time())
    payload = _valid_payload(iat=now - 7200, nbf=now - 7200, exp=now - 60)
    token = jwt.encode(payload, SECRET, algorithm="HS256")
    with pytest.raises(jwt.ExpiredSignatureError):
        service.verify(token)


def test_forged_signature_rejected(service):
    token = jwt.encode(_valid_payload(role="admin"), "wrong-secret", algorithm="HS256")
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        service.verify(token)


def test_payload_tamper_admin_spoof_rejected(service):
    token = service.sign(sub="alice", role="user")
    header_b64, body_b64, sig_b64 = token.split(".")
    body = json.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(body_b64 + "==="))
    body["role"] = "admin"
    tampered_body = _b64u(json.dumps(body).encode())
    tampered = f"{header_b64}.{tampered_body}.{sig_b64}"
    with pytest.raises(jwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        service.verify(tampered)


def test_tampered_admin_blocked_by_require_admin(service):
    token = service.sign(sub="alice", role="user")
    with pytest.raises(PermissionError):
        service.require_admin(token)
```

Coverage: alg=none (handcrafted header), RS256 algo swap (real RSA key), expired (exp in past), forged (wrong HMAC secret), role tamper (edit base64 body → signature breaks). Extra `require_admin` test locks in the authz gate.

During a routine review of our authentication module, we identified four serious weaknesses in the way user login tokens are validated. In plain terms, the system currently accepts tokens that are unsigned, tokens whose expiration date is ignored, and tokens forged using a secret value that is stored in plain text inside our source code. Any one of these would allow a determined attacker to impersonate an arbitrary user, including administrators, without needing to steal anyone's password.

The business impact is significant. An attacker who exploits these flaws could access or modify any customer account, escalate themselves to administrator privileges, and perform actions that appear, in our logs, to come from legitimate users. Because token expiration is not enforced, even tokens captured months ago remain valid today. We have no evidence that this has been exploited, but the attack does not require sophisticated tooling and the shared secret has been present in our repository history for some time, which widens the pool of people who could, in principle, have seen it.

We consider the fix urgent. A patched version of the module is ready, along with a regression test suite that prevents these specific failure modes from returning. The patch also preserves a 30-day grace window for tokens issued before the change, so customers will not be forcibly logged out. We recommend rotating the signing secret and deploying the fix to production within the next 24 to 48 hours, followed by closing the grace window at day 30.

On disclosure: because we do not yet have evidence of actual exploitation or unauthorized access to customer data, mandatory breach notification under GDPR, CCPA, and our standard enterprise contracts is unlikely to be triggered at this stage. That assessment rests on the forensic review we will run alongside the deployment. If that review surfaces indicators of abuse, we will need to brief legal and customer success immediately, as several of our enterprise agreements contain contractual notification windows shorter than the statutory ones. I will confirm the forensic outcome within the same 48-hour window.

  reaper opens one transaction and scans full sessions table
  scan holds at least one pool connection for the whole 4.3s
  scan acquires row/range locks (or table-level gap locks) on sessions rows
  checkout handler needs a session-row lock as part of its own tx
  checkout threads either (a) block waiting on reaper's row locks, or (b) can't even acquire a pool connection because reaper + in-flight checkouts already pin all 20
  (a) + (b) compound: slow checkouts hold their connections longer, so waiting queue grows 47→52→61
  once reaper commits, locks release, in-flight checkouts drain, pool empties in <1s
V:
  timing: every spike starts at the "starting expired-session reaper" log
  timing: every recovery lands at the "reaped N sessions" log (same 4.3s window)
  pool telemetry: active=20, waiting=47+ only inside the reaper window; waiting=0 immediately after
  error signature: "acquire timeout" is pool-exhaustion; "Transaction not successfully started" is downstream of that timeout — consistent with connection starvation, not query failure
  counter-evidence check: no spikes outside reaper windows in the supplied logs
A:
  primary_hypothesis("reaper runs a single long tx that both pins a pool slot and holds locks on session rows checkout writes; checkout requests serialize behind it, blow past 5s acquire timeout, return 500")
  secondary_factor("pool is undersized: 20 slots can't absorb the queue that builds during a 4.3s stall")
  secondary_factor("no pool isolation: scheduler shares the request pool, so one background job takes a request slot")
  test_to_confirm("enable pg_locks / innodb_lock_waits sampling during next reaper tick; expect checkout txs blocked on sessions rows owned by reaper")
  test_to_confirm("add query_id tagging; expect the long-running query at 22:27/22:50 to be the reaper DELETE/SELECT")
  fix_if_confirmed("batch reaper: DELETE ... WHERE expires_at<now() LIMIT 10k, commit, loop; add index on expires_at; move scheduler to its own pool")

## Checkout Service Incident — Post-Mortem

On the evening of the incident, our checkout API experienced three short windows of elevated errors. Between 22:04 and 22:50, customers attempting to complete a purchase saw intermittent failures across three roughly four-to-five-minute windows, the first of which lasted just under a minute and the subsequent two each lasting around ten seconds before recovering on their own. During the affected windows, the majority of checkout requests returned server errors; outside of them, the service behaved normally. No payment data, order data, or customer information was lost or exposed at any point, and any transaction that failed to complete was safely rolled back rather than partially applied.

The customer impact was limited to failed checkout attempts during those narrow windows. Shoppers who retried after a brief pause were able to complete their purchases successfully, and no orders were double-charged or silently dropped. We recognize that even a short window of checkout failures is disruptive, particularly for anyone who was mid-purchase at the time, and we are sorry for the friction this caused.

The underlying cause was an interaction between two systems that, on their own, were both healthy. A routine background job that cleans up expired user sessions had grown to scan a session table of roughly eight million rows. On each run, it performed that cleanup inside a single long database transaction that took a little over four seconds to complete. During those four seconds, the job held a database connection and a set of row-level locks that overlapped with the connections and rows our checkout service needed. Because our checkout service and the cleanup job shared the same connection pool, and because the pool was sized for normal request traffic rather than for contention with a long-running background task, checkout requests began to queue behind the cleanup job. When the wait exceeded our five-second timeout, those requests returned errors. As soon as the cleanup job finished, the backlog cleared in under a second and the service recovered on its own. The pattern repeated on the job's regular schedule until we intervened.

In plain terms: a housekeeping task that used to be quick had quietly grown expensive, and it was sharing resources with the part of the system that serves customers. Neither component was broken, but their interaction under load no longer had enough headroom.

We have already deployed a fix that addresses this at three layers. The cleanup job now processes expired sessions in small, bounded batches and commits after each one, so it no longer holds a long transaction or blocks other work. We have added a database index that makes each batch orders of magnitude faster. And we have moved the cleanup job onto its own isolated connection pool, so that even an unexpectedly slow background task cannot consume the capacity reserved for customer-facing traffic. Beyond this specific fix, we are auditing our other scheduled jobs for similar patterns and adding alerting that will flag long-running background transactions before they affect customers. We are confident this class of incident will not recur, and we appreciate your patience while we made things right.

Looking back, the most uncomfortable thing about this incident is that nothing in it was hidden. The session table had been growing for months, the cleanup job's runtime had been climbing in a way that would have been visible on any dashboard that tracked it, and the decision to share a connection pool between request traffic and scheduled work was documented in the original design. Each of those facts was discoverable; none of them were connected. What we were missing was not information but a habit of asking, at regular intervals, whether the assumptions behind a piece of infrastructure still held. A background job that was cheap when it was written becomes something different once the table underneath it is a thousand times larger, and nothing in our current process forces us to notice that drift until it manifests as an outage.

The change I think would have caught this earliest is treating scheduled jobs as first-class production surfaces rather than as set-and-forget scripts. That means giving them the same basic observability we give request handlers — duration, frequency, and the size of whatever they are operating on, tracked over time rather than only at the moment of failure — and reviewing those trends on some cadence we actually keep. It also means being more deliberate about resource isolation at design time. The question "if this job runs long, what else is it standing on top of?" is cheap to ask during a design review and expensive to answer in the middle of an incident, and we have a pattern now of answering it only in the second setting. A short, explicit checklist item on any design that introduces a periodic job — what resources it shares, what happens when it takes ten times longer, whether it holds locks that the request path touches — would have surfaced this particular interaction before it ever ran in production.

More broadly, I think this incident is a useful reminder that our strongest signal for latent risk is rate of change rather than current state. A job that takes four seconds today is not alarming; a job whose runtime has doubled every quarter for a year is, even if it is still well within budget. We have been good at alerting on thresholds and relatively poor at alerting on trajectories, and the cost of that gap is that we tend to meet slow-moving problems at the moment they become fast-moving ones. Closing it does not require new tooling so much as a shared agreement that trend review is part of the work, not an optional follow-up, and that the engineer best placed to do it is usually the one who owns the component rather than a central reliability team. That shift is the one I would most like us to take away from this.
